explanation: SET NOCOUNT OFF leaves row-count messages enabled, adding unnecessary network chatter; use SET NOCOUNT ON.
SET XACT_ABORT OFF allows some run-time errors to leave transactions partially applied; enable it when transactions are used.
SELECT * on Sales.Orders couples callers to every current and future column.
SELECT TOP (10) on Sales.Orders has no ORDER BY, returning an arbitrary set of rows.
DISTINCT on o.OrderID is redundant because OrderID is the primary key and already unique.
WITH (NOLOCK) on Sales.Orders can return uncommitted, missing, or duplicated rows.
YEAR(o.OrderDate) applies a function to the date column, preventing an index range seek.
Comparing int column o.CustomerID with nvarchar @CustomerIdText forces an implicit conversion.
The @CustomerID IS NULL OR o.CustomerID = @CustomerID pattern defeats cardinality estimation and seeking.
LIKE N'%urgent%' has a leading wildcard, preventing an index seek on Comments.
NOT IN against Sales.Invoices.CustomerID yields surprising results if the subquery returns NULL.
ISNULL(o.CustomerID, 0) = 1 wraps the column, making the predicate non-sargable.
o.OrderID + 0 = @OrderID performs arithmetic on the column, preventing index matching.
o.CustomerID <> 1 is a negative predicate that often scans broad portions of the table.
SELECT TOP (1) without ORDER BY returns arbitrary rows from the cross join.
CROSS JOIN of Sales.Orders and Sales.Customers has no join predicate and multiplies rows.
Comma-separated FROM with join condition in WHERE uses deprecated old-style join syntax.
SELECT TOP (1) without ORDER BY returns arbitrary rows.
The correlated COUNT(*) subquery executes a scalar lookup per outer order row; use a join/aggregate.
COUNT(*) inside the scalar subquery counts all rows though only a count-per-order is needed.
The correlated EXISTS against Sales.OrderLines could be expressed as a set join.
The CASE WHEN o.Comments IS NULL expression is repeated in the SELECT list and ORDER BY.
ORDER BY 2 references a select-list position, becoming fragile if the column list changes.
OFFSET/FETCH ordered only by o.OrderDate lacks a stable tie-breaker, yielding nondeterministic pages.
UNION between disjoint OrderID=1 and OrderID=2 branches performs needless duplicate removal; use UNION ALL.
(SELECT COUNT(*) ...) > 0 counts all matching rows when EXISTS would suffice.
The correlated COUNT(*) subquery runs per outer order row.
SELECT @OrderID = o.OrderID silently accepts multiple rows and keeps the last value.
The @OrderIds table variable may receive poor cardinality estimates for non-trivial row counts.
SELECT ... INTO #ImplicitOrders creates the temp table schema implicitly instead of declaring it.
SELECT TOP (5) INTO without ORDER BY selects an arbitrary set of rows.
A cursor is used for work that could be expressed set-wise.
SELECT TOP (2) in the cursor declaration has no ORDER BY.
The WHILE loop performs iterative work instead of a set-based operation.
SQL text is built by concatenating @CustomerID into the executable string.
EXEC (@Sql) executes dynamic SQL without parameterization.
EXEC (@Sql) is fine, but the built statement is unqualified; prefer parameterized sp_executesql.
The @OrderID value is concatenated into the SQL string rather than bound as a parameter.
sp_executesql is called but the value is embedded in the text rather than passed as a parameter.
#Scratch is explicitly created but never dropped before the procedure ends.
The CATCH block selects ERROR_MESSAGE() but neither rethrows with THROW nor returns a failure status.
This CATCH block handles an error without rolling back any active transaction.
RAISERROR is used for a new error path; use THROW instead.
PRINT is buffered and unsuitable for structured diagnostics.
o.CustomerID = 1 embeds a business identifier instead of accepting it as a parameter.
o.OrderDate >= '20160101' embeds an unlabelled date constant in query logic.
CONVERT(date, '01/02/2016') has no style parameter, relying on session-sensitive interpretation.
COALESCE on both sides of the join predicate inhibits normal join optimization.
SELECT TOP (1) without ORDER BY returns an arbitrary row.
Self-joins Sales.Orders on OrderID to retrieve the same key already available in the row.
SELECT TOP (1) without ORDER BY returns an arbitrary row.
ORDER BY o.Comments sorts by an unselective nvarchar(MAX) column.
SUM(ol.Quantity) aggregates the entire Sales.OrderLines table with no restrictive filter.
HAVING ol.OrderID > 0 is a row-level predicate that belongs in WHERE, not HAVING.
ORDER BY on a single-row COUNT(*) aggregate result is unnecessary.
MERGE has documented concurrency and correctness edge cases; use separate IF EXISTS/UPDATE/INSERT blocks.
WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET target.Comments = target.Comments changes no data but still logs and locks.
UPDATE SET Comments = Comments assigns the current value, causing logging and locking with no change.
DELETE FROM Sales.OrderLines WHERE Quantity = -1 uses a broad non-key predicate.
WAITFOR DELAY blocks a worker thread instead of using asynchronous scheduling.
BEGIN TRANSACTION is started outside a TRY/CATCH error-handling boundary.
SET IDENTITY_INSERT Sales.Orders ON changes session state and creates fragile identity coupling.
EXEC sp_who calls a system routine without schema qualification and using the reserved sp_ prefix.
SET ROWCOUNT 1 changes a session setting and is never restored.
INSERT INTO #DemoCategories VALUES (...) omits an explicit column list.
The literal 99 CategoryID is embedded directly in the VALUES clause.
INSERT INTO #DemoCustomers via SELECT lacks an explicit target column list.
LIKE '%' + @CustomerName + '%' has a leading wildcard preventing an index seek.
The SQL string is built by concatenating @CustomerName into executable text.
User-supplied @CustomerName is concatenated directly into dynamic SQL, enabling SQL injection.
The dynamic LIKE '%...%' pattern has a leading wildcard preventing an index seek.
sp_executesql executes the concatenated string without any parameter binding.
UPDATE #DemoCustomers SET CustomerCategoryID = 1 has no WHERE clause and updates all rows.
CustomerCategoryID = 1 embeds a magic category value in the UPDATE.
WHERE o.OrderDate >= '2016-01-01' on the LEFT JOINed Sales.Orders converts the outer join to an inner join.
The date constant '2016-01-01' is embedded unlabelled in query logic.
WHERE DeliveryMethodID = NULL never matches; use IS NULL instead.
dbo.GetCustomerType(CustomerID) is a scalar UDF in the SELECT list, forcing row-by-row evaluation.
The CATCH block for the transaction does not roll back the active DELETE transaction before handling the error.
The CATCH block selects ERROR_MESSAGE() but neither rethrows nor returns a failure status.
WITH (INDEX = 1) hardcodes an index hint in production code.
LIKE @CustomerName may contain a leading wildcard, preventing an index seek.
Three levels of nested derived tables should be refactored to CTEs for readability.
LIKE '%A%' has a leading wildcard preventing an index seek.
SELECT * from the table-valued function couples the caller to all output columns.
dbo.GetCustomerOrdersMulti appears to be a multi-statement TVF; an inline TVF would be better optimized.
